what_it_does: Records a posted note by Tim that acknowledges findings about the mobile Safari long-press selection-vs-scroll conflict and states they should be added to requirements.
observations: It is a note of type 'note' in the dragnet-development channel, posted by author_session 'tim' and linked as a comment on item-89d62e5d.
The body describes the iOS long-press conflict between native text-selection callout and page scrolling.
It prescribes preventDefault on touchstart/selectstart inside the annotatable surface, deliberate toggling of -webkit-user-select, and -webkit-touch-callout: none.
It emphasizes real-device-only verification, specifically on the user's actual device, and references 'BD-I'.
The note ends with an explicit requirement-capture intent: 'this kind of stuff will definitely need to be added to whatever requirements.'
standouts: Explicit call to convert findings into formal requirements.
Strong emphasis on real-device verification rather than desktop browser testing.
notes: A short requirement-capture note preserving mobile Safari long-press interaction findings from a prior discussion.
questions: What is the content and context of the parent item item-89d62e5d?
Which specific product or feature do these requirements apply to?
What is the target requirements document or tracker?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file is a posted note that records a point and signals it should become a requirement.
intention_signals: Preserve the discovered mobile Safari long-press behavior and ensure it is formalized as an implementation requirement.
contribution: Captures a precise mobile Safari interaction constraint and the intent to document it, preserving domain knowledge needed for implementing touch-based annotation without native callout interference.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard note by Tim commenting on mobile Safari long-press selection-vs-scroll conflicts and stating the findings must be added to requirements.
intention_for_embedding: To record and escalate mobile Safari gesture-handling findings into formal requirements.
